steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question assumes P7 gave equal actual ratings to impressionist and color-field paintings, then asks what the model predicted. If P7's actual ratings were equal but the model's predicted ratings differ for the two styles, there is a mismatch between prediction and reality — that mismatch is what the data show.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'P7’s ratings for impressionist and color-field paintings would differ from one another.': The graph shows the model assigned different predicted scores to P7 for the two styles. This directly matches the setup. ✓
  • Choice B — 'P7 would derive more aesthetic pleasure from impressionist paintings than from color-field paintings.': This claims a specific direction (impressionist > color-field). The graph shows the predicted scores differ, but the question does not specify which is higher for P7 — this goes beyond what the data show. ✗
  • Choice C — 'P7’s ratings for impressionist and color-field paintings would equal one another.': This says the model predicted equal scores, but the graph shows the predicted values for P7 are not equal. This is the opposite of what the graph indicates. ✗
  • Choice D — 'P7 would derive less aesthetic pleasure from impressionist paintings than from color-field paintings.': Like B, this asserts a specific directional difference (color-field > impressionist) that the graph does not confirm. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
